Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd tell myself I'd study after work or after hanging out with friends, but it rarely happened the way I planned. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, usually late at night when I suddenly remembered something was due. I'd sit down with good intentions but end up scrolling on my phone or watching something instead. The 21 hours I supposedly studied each week were probably more like half actual studying and half just having materials open while doing other things.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I work part-time and have a pretty active social life with my extracurriculars, so studying always got squeezed into whatever time was left. Deadlines were really the only thing that forced me to sit down and actually focus. Some weeks I'd do almost nothing, then I'd cram everything into two or three days before something was due. It was exhausting but I couldn't seem to break the cycle.
Q3: Usually it was panic. Like, I'd suddenly remember an assignment was coming up or I'd get an email notification about something. Sometimes I logged in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which I often had. Occasionally I'd log in because I was bored, but then I'd see all the work I needed to do and just close the tab. It wasn't exactly a motivating experience.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a week or two early in the semester where I tried to check in daily, but that fell apart pretty quickly. The LMS just felt like this overwhelming list of things I was behind on. Every time I logged in, I'd see all these modules and deadlines and it just made me anxious. So I started avoiding it, which obviously made things worse.
Q5: The recorded lectures were probably the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could play them while doing other things or during my commute. The discussion posts were sometimes helpful too, because I could see how other people understood the material. But honestly, I didn't engage with most resources as much as I should have. When I did use something, it was usually because I absolutely had to.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the readings and the supplementary materials. They just felt like too much effort, especially when I was already feeling behind. The discussion forums I mostly avoided because I didn't feel like I had anything smart to contribute. I'd skim things and tell myself I'd come back to them later, but later never really came. It all just piled up until it felt impossible to catch up.
Q7: If I could see an immediate connection to an assignment or exam, I'd actually do it. Like, if I knew watching a specific video would help me answer a quiz question, I'd watch it. But anything that felt vague or optional just didn't seem worth the effort when I had so many other things competing for my time. I needed to see the payoff right away or I'd lose interest.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. I'd see it and think 'that's nice' and then never look at it again. Required activities at least had some urgency attached to them. Even then, I'd often do the bare minimum just to get credit. The optional stuff felt like extra work for no reward, especially when I was already struggling to keep up with what was mandatory.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a bunch of lecture slides and PDFs early on and would sometimes look at them on my phone when I was out. I also used YouTube videos and other websites to understand concepts that weren't clicking from the course materials. Some of my friends from extracurriculars were in similar courses, so we'd sometimes talk through stuff together. None of that showed up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were times I'd listen to downloaded lectures while commuting or working out, just absorbing information without any way to track it. Or I'd be texting with a classmate about an assignment, working through problems together, but neither of us was logged into anything. I also did a lot of thinking about course concepts while doing other things, like trying to connect ideas while I was at work. That kind of passive processing doesn't leave any digital footprint.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download something once, maybe watch the first few minutes online, then finish it offline later. Or I'd screenshot important slides and review them on my phone throughout the week. The LMS would only show that initial access, not the multiple times I actually looked at the material afterward. It was just more convenient to have things saved locally.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart for me. I was dealing with increased hours at work, plus my anxiety was really spiking. I'd sit down to study and just feel this overwhelming sense of dread. The CBT sessions I started helped with my mood eventually, but by then I'd already fallen so far behind that catching up felt impossible. There were weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to even open the LMS.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired from work or stressed about other things, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. If I was confused about something, I'd often just give up rather than try to figure it out. The competing responsibilities were the worst part—I'd have to choose between studying, working, and my extracurriculars, and honestly, studying usually lost. It felt less immediately important even though I knew it wasn't.
Q14: So many times. I'd be logged in, maybe have a video playing, but my mind would be completely elsewhere. I'd realize I'd watched twenty minutes without actually processing anything. Or I'd scroll through readings, highlighting things randomly, without really understanding what I was reading. It was like going through the motions without actually being present. My body was there but my brain had checked out.
Q15: All the time. I knew certain assignments were important but I'd keep pushing them off because they felt too big or too hard. The anxiety would build up the more I avoided them, which made me want to avoid them even more. It was this terrible cycle where I knew I was making things worse but couldn't seem to stop. Sometimes I'd do easier, less important tasks just to feel like I was accomplishing something.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually do work. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly go into crisis mode, trying to cram everything in at the last minute. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates and then dropped to almost nothing afterward. It wasn't healthy, and the quality of my work suffered, but it was the only way I could motivate myself to actually engage.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially negative feedback, it honestly just made me feel worse and less motivated to try. I'd see a bad grade and think 'what's the point' instead of using it to improve. The few times I got encouraging comments, I felt a brief boost, but it didn't last. I think I needed more immediate feedback to stay engaged, but by the time I got responses, I'd already moved on mentally.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming to me. There were so many modules and so many different types of activities that I never felt like I had a clear path forward. I'd log in and not know where to start, so I'd just pick something random or nothing at all. A more linear structure with clearer priorities might have helped me stay on track. Instead, it felt like I was constantly drowning in options.
Q19: The sheer amount of content made engagement harder. Every time I logged in, there was more stuff to do, which triggered my anxiety. The lack of clear deadlines for some materials meant I'd keep postponing them indefinitely. What would have helped is maybe smaller, more frequent checkpoints instead of big assignments. The discussion requirements also felt forced, which made me less likely to participate meaningfully.
Q20: That was kind of me, honestly. I'd log in, click around, watch parts of videos, start things without finishing them. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just wandering around the platform trying to figure out what to do or procrastinating by looking at materials without actually working on assignments. The system counts clicks, not comprehension or actual work produced.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone already knows the material or learns efficiently outside the LMS, they don't need to spend hours clicking around. They might download everything once, study offline, and just show up for assessments. I tried to do that but I wasn't disciplined enough to make it work. Some people are just better at independent learning than I am.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when you're active, how often you log in, what you click on. But they can't show whether you actually understood anything or if you were just going through the motions. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing, the conversations with classmates. They also can't capture why you're disengaged—whether it's boredom, anxiety, confusion, or life circumstances. The numbers tell a story but it's not the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just complete it. It's when you're curious about the material and it doesn't feel like a chore. For me, I rarely felt that this semester. I was mostly just trying to survive and meet minimum requirements. Real engagement would mean I'm thinking about the content even when I don't have to, which definitely wasn't happening.
Q24: The data wouldn't show how much I was struggling mentally this semester. It wouldn't capture the anxiety attacks before logging in or the shame I felt about falling behind. It also wouldn't show that I actually found some of the content interesting when I could get past the stress. My extracurriculars and work situation, the CBT sessions I started—none of that context appears in login data. I'm more than just my clicks and completion rates.
